coredata.dc:title: Employing carbon dots modified with vancomycin for assaying Gram-positive bacteria like Staphylococcus aureus
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhong, Dan
Zhuo, Yan
Feng, Yuanjiao
Yang, Xiaoming
coredata.dc:description: By employing attractive performance of fluorescent carbon dots, we herein successfully established an assay for analyzing bacteria firstly. Specifically, carbon dots with blue fluorescence were initially synthesized according to a previous report, and modified with vancomycin on their surfaces. Subsequently, the prepared carbon dots were applied to detect Staphylococcus aureus accompanied with a linear range of 3.18×105–1.59×108 cfu/mL as well as a detection limit of 9.40×104 cfu/mL. Compared with other regular methods, our method is more rapid and convenient in term of methodology. Meanwhile, the current strategy was applied for detection of other bacteria including Bacillus subtilis, Listeria monocytogenes, Salmonella, Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Escherichia coli, and the modified carbon dots showed obvious affinity with Gram-positive bacteria owing to the ligand–receptor interactions between vancomycin and the cell walls, suggesting its value for detecting Gram-positive bacteria. Additionally, the practicability of this sensing approach was validated by recovery experiments conducted in orange juice, confirming its potential to broaden avenues for detection of Gram-positive bacteria.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Vancomycin
Assaying
Gram-positive bacteria

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CDs modified with vancomycin
Detecting target bacteria in actual samples
Building up this assay and its mechanism
Detection of S. aureus by using CD@Van
Analysis S. aureus in actual sample

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge financial support by Research Fund for the Doctoral Program of Higher Education of China ( 20110182120014 ), Natural Science Foundation Project of CQ CSTC ( cstc2013jcyjA10117 ), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities ( XDJK2015A005 and 2362014xk07 ), and Program for Innovative Research Team in University of Chongqing (2013). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of our method for detecting Staphylococcus aureus.

(A) Fluorescence excitation (a) and emission (b) spectra of CDs, Inset: photographs of CDs under daylight (I) and UV light (II); (B) FTIR spectrum of CDs; (C) HR-TEM image of CDs; (D) Dynamic light scattering (DLS) histogram of CDs.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of CDs (black) and CD@Van (red), Inset: photographs of silica powder loaded with CDs (I, III) or CD@Van (II, IV) under daylight (I, II) and UV light (III, IV); (B) FTIR spectrum of CD@Van; (C) HR-TEM image of CD@Van; (D) MS spectra of CD@Van. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Fluorescence spectra of CD@Van in the absence (black) and presence (red) of 3.26×107 cfu/mL S. aureus; (B) Fluorescence spectra of CDs in the presence of different concentration of S. aureus; (C) and (D) HR-TEM images of CD@Van aggregating on S. aureus. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Fluorescence spectra of CD@Van in the presence of varied concentrations of S. aureus (A), Bacillus subtilis (C) and L. monocytogenes (E); Plot of the fluorescence intensity versus the logarithm of concentrations of S. aureus (B), Bacillus subtilis (D) and L. monocytogenes (E) added.
Recoveries of S. aureus growing in orange juice directly detected by the proposed method (n=6).

head.ce:title: Employing carbon dots modified with vancomycin for assaying Gram-positive bacteria like Staphylococcus aureus

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Key Laboratory of Luminescence and Real-Time Analytical Chemistry, Southwest University, Ministry of Education, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Key Laboratory of Luminescence and Real-Time Analytical Chemistry, Southwest University, Ministry of Education, Southwest University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: By employing attractive performance of fluorescent carbon dots, we herein successfully established an assay for analyzing bacteria firstly. Specifically, carbon dots with blue fluorescence were initially synthesized according to a previous report, and modified with vancomycin on their surfaces. Subsequently, the prepared carbon dots were applied to detect Staphylococcus aureus accompanied with a linear range of 3.18×105–1.59×108 cfu/mL as well as a detection limit of 9.40×104 cfu/mL. Compared with other regular methods, our method is more rapid and convenient in term of methodology. Meanwhile, the current strategy was applied for detection of other bacteria including Bacillus subtilis, Listeria monocytogenes, Salmonella, Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Escherichia coli, and the modified carbon dots showed obvious affinity with Gram-positive bacteria owing to the ligand–receptor interactions between vancomycin and the cell walls, suggesting its value for detecting Gram-positive bacteria. Additionally, the practicability of this sensing approach was validated by recovery experiments conducted in orange juice, confirming its potential to broaden avenues for detection of Gram-positive bacteria.

ce:section.ce:para: Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus), belonging to a member of Gram-positive bacteria, is also well known as a virulent pathogen blamed for its highly stabile toxic proteins mainly including different staphylococcal enterotoxins and the toxic shock syndrome toxin (Arbuthnott et al., 1990; Balasubramanian et al., 2007). More importantly, S. aureus can cause diverse diseases such as pneumonia, pericarditis, urinary tract infections and even septicemia. Such infections could be lethal with improper medical treatment, so it has been considered to be one of the most serious risks in the public health all around the word. Remarkably, S. aureus is temperature (7–48°C) and pH (4.2–9.3) tolerant and can survive in hypersaline environment (Jia et al., 2014; Pividori and Alegret, 2010; Wang et al., 2011a). Especially, foods like milk, dairy products and meat products are easily contaminated by S. aureus once treated in unclean environment. Hence, quantification detection of S. aureus exhibits great significance for human safety. Currently, various efforts have been devoted to explore convenient and accurate approaches for identifying bacteria (Chan and Chen, 2012; Chen et al., 2008b; Lin et al., 2013; Xie et al., 2012; Yang et al., 2010), but each of them showed its own drawbacks. On the basis of routine bacterial culture and isolation technology, bacterial enrichment in medium followed by plate counting served as the traditional and most common method (Jia et al., 2014), and was obviously limited by time consuming and low sensitivity. Another common method by virtue of commercial optical scanning devices is also time-consuming (24h) for incubation and is not suitable for clinical diagnosis, although it can provide satisfactory detections of bacteria (Gao et al., 2006). Besides, the detection technique originated from real-time fluorescent quantitative PCR (FQ-PCR) has been employed for detecting the fluorescence of specific nucleic acids in S. aureus. However, the similar disadvantages of requiring highly skilled operators and expensive equipment, being prone to contamination and providing indirect relationship between the results and concentrations of S. aureus also limited its applications (Ding et al., 2011; Thet et al., 2013). Consequently, traditional assays towards pathogen are obviously defective, lead to the necessity of developing rapid and sensitive strategies.

Carbon dots (CDs) are fascinating carbon materials owing to their unique properties consisting of stable photoluminescence, tunable excitation and emission wavelength, lack of optical blinking, small size, low toxicity, favorable biocompatibility and satisfactory fluorescent performance (Baker and Baker, 2010; Wang et al., 2011b; Yang et al., 2009a). Hence, this kind of material have drawn considerable attentions in the fields of nanobiotechnology, biological labeling, photocatalysis (Cao et al., 2011; Li et al., 2010), sensing (Zhao et al., 2011; Zhou et al., 2012), biomedicine (Wang et al., 2011a) and delivering drugs (Lin et al., 2005; Yang et al., 2009b). In the past few years, two major approaches have been developed for synthesizing CDs. One way was known as the top down, consisting of electrochemical oxidation (Zhao et al., 2008; Zhou et al., 2007), acidic oxidation (Dong et al., 2010), arc discharge (Xu et al., 2004) and laser ablation (Hu et al., 2009). For the other way, hydrothermal (Zhu et al., 2012), microwave (Salinas-Castillo et al., 2013) and ultrasonic (Zhuo et al., 2012) defined as the bottom up, have been basically applied to synthesize CDs. Compared with conventional organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) that are seriously pernicious for human health and the environment, CDs exhibited fascinating properties and potentiality not only in theoretic but also in practical aspects. Especially, numerous CDs have been utilized to design fluorescent probes towards analysis purpose (Algarra et al., 2014; da Silva and Goncalves 2011; Goncalves et al., 2010).

Serving as one of glycopeptide antibiotics, vancomycin (Van) usually functions as a broad-spectrum antibiotic, and was first isolated from a soil sample collected by an American pharmaceutical company of Eli Lilly in the mid-1950s (Williams and Bardsley, 1999). Currently, the vancomycin group antibiotics have been propelled to the front of fighting against the serious pathogen like S. aureus and MRSA (methicillin resistant Staphylococcus aureus) on the basis of their clinical effect for treating Gram-positive bacterial infection (Xing et al., 2002). By forming hydrogen bonds, vancomycin can bind to the terminal peptide of d-Ala-d-Ala on the cell wall of the Gram-positive bacterium (Dancer et al., 1996; Gu et al., 2003; Liu et al., 2011; Williams and Bardsley, 1999). In fact, substantial contributions have been devoted to test bacteria by utilizing the broad affinity between vancomycin (Gao et al., 2006; Gu et al., 2003; Lin et al., 2005; Qi et al., 2013), lectins (Afonso and Fenselau, 2003; Bundy and Fenselau 1999; Bundy and Fenselau, 2001), immunoglobulin G (Chen et al., 2008a; Ho et al., 2004) and bacteria.

Inspired by the attractive fluorescent performance of CDs and the interactions bacteria binding with vancomycin, we hereby established a facile approach for fast and accurate detection of Gram-positive bacteria originated from CDs modified with vancomycin ( Fig. 1). Specifically, the modified CDs aggregated on the surfaces of bacteria owing to the interactions between vancomycin and bacteria, thereby facilitating the decrease of CDs' fluorescence. In contrast with traditional methods, this solution largely shortened the detection time to less than 1h and allowed the whole process with scarce interference from complex samples. Accordingly, we have provided a promising opportunity to help overcome the difficulties faced by conventional detection methods, which generally requires time consuming and sophisticated operations (Liu et al., 2011). Additionally, the practicability of this sensing approach was validated by recovery experiments of detecting S. aureus in orange juice, and the results demonstrated its potential for analyzing Gram-positive bacteria existing in multi-matrix. Meaningfully, the current strategy by means of carbon dots conjugated with vancomycin (CD@Van) for detecting Gram-positive bacteria was reported for the first time.

N-hydroxysulfosuccinimide (NHS) and 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Milwaukee, USA). Vancomycin hydrochloride was obtained from Shanghai Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Disodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4), sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4), citric acid, urea, sodium chloride (NaCl) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were purchased from Dingguo Changsheng Biotech Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). Tryptone, beef extract, peptone, yeast extract powder, ager were obtained from Beijing Aoboxing Biotech Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). S. aureus, Salmonella, Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Escherichia coli were acquired as gifts from microbiology laboratory, College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University (Chongqing, China). Bacillus subtilis, Listeria monocytogenes (L. monocytogenes) were purchased from China General Microbiological Culture Collection Center. Ultrapure water of 18.25MΩcm, produced with an Aquapro AWL-0502-P ultrapure water system (Chongqing, China), was employed for the following experiments.

All fluorescence measurements were performed on a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan) with excitation slit set at 5nm band pass and emission at 5nm band pass in 1cm×1cm quartz cell. High resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) images were taken via a TECNAI G2 F20 microscope (FEI, America) at 200KV. Meanwhile, UV/vis absorption spectra were recorded by a Shimadzu UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). Functional groups analysis was achieved by Fourier Transform Infrared spectrometer (Tokyo, Japan). Mass spectra were obtained by UltrafleXtreme Matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization-time-of-flight mass spectrometry (MALDI TOF/TOF MS, Bruker Daltonics Inc., USA). A Fangzhong pHS-3C digital pH meter (Chengdu, China) was used to measure various pH values of the aqueous solutions, and a vortex mixer QL-901 (Haimen, China) for blending kinds of solutions. All the bacterial growth and assays were performed on a SW-CJ-1FD super-clean bench (Suzhou, China).

The CDs here were synthesized according to a previous work (Qu et al., 2012). Typically, citric acid (3g) and urea (3g) first were added into distilled water (10mL) to form a transparent solution. Then the solution was heated in a domestic 750W microwave until the color of the solution changed from colorless to yellow or dark yellow, thus the CDs were produced. Subsequently, this product was dissolved in distilled water (20mL), and subjected to centrifugation (5000rpm, 5min) to remove large particles, and 300 MWCO of dialysis membrane applied for purification. The powder of CDs was obtained by lyophilisation, and further dissolved in ultrapure water with the final concentration of 100mg/mL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs modified with vancomycin
ce:section.ce:para: In brief, the CDs solution were first diluted to 80 folds by (10mM, pH 6) phosphate buffer (PB), then 10mg of fresh EDC and NHS were successively introduced into 2mL of the prepared CDs for activating carboxyl groups of their surfaces, and followed by reacting with rotation for 4h at room temperature. Next, 20mg vancomycin was dissolved in 2mL (20mM, pH 7.4) phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), forming a transparent solution. Simultaneously, 2mL of vancomycin solution was added to this CDs solution, and further gently stirred for 6h at room temperature. Ultimately, 1500 MWCO of dialysis membrane were applied to remove free vancomycin and CDs.

Live bacteria were directly subjected to the following experiments in this contribution. Initially, S. aureus, B. subtilis, Salmonella, P. aeruginosa and E. coli grew in sterile Luria–Bertani (LB) media (2.0g bacto-tryptone, 1.0g bacto-yeast extract powder, 2.0g NaCl dissolving in 200mL distilled water, and adjusted to pH 7.4 with 3.0M NaOH) in an incubator–shaker at 37°C to reach the growing stationary phase. After overnight incubation, 1mL of bacterial cells was centrifuged (6000rpm, 10min) to remove the supernatant and washed 3 times with PBS (20mM, pH 7.4), and resuspended in 1mL of PBS. Howbeit, L. monocytogenes grew in Nutrient–Broth (2.0g bacto-peptone, 0.6g bacto-beef extract, 1.0g NaCl dissolving in 200mL distilled water, and adjusted to pH 7.4 with 3.0M NaOH). The bacterium concentration was adjusted to an appropriate level by measuring the optical density (OD) at 600nm. By plating kinds of bacteria on LB or NB plates, the amounts of bacteria per milliliter can be acquired by counting related colony forming units (cfu) after incubation overnight at 37°C. Towards the purpose of detecting, bacteria samples were diluted into various concentrations by using PBS buffer. Then 500µL CD@Van was mixed respectively with 500µL each sample described here, and incubated for 50min, and fluorescence variations of CD@Van were further recorded by FL spectrometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detecting target bacteria in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate its recovery of the raised method, the orange juice bought from Yonghui supermarket (Chongqing, China) was introduced, and its impurities were separated by centrifugation (3000rpm, 5min) at the very beginning. Then small amount of S. aureus were spiked into the orange juice and cultured at different time to obtain various concentrations of bacterial suspension. Ultimately, the prepared samples were used for bacteria detection based on the general procedure without additionally special treatment. Besides, the traditional plate-counting method for assaying the bacteria supplemented in orange juice served as the controls.

Basically, the CDs described here served as a foundation of the following detection strategy, thus all the work started with characterizing this CDs. Firstly, the maximum excitation and emission spectra of the synthesized CDs were recorded as 320nm and 435nm ( Fig. 2A) respectively, and their fluorescent properties were subsequently explored. To be specific, the CDs solution emitted obvious blue fluorescence (photograph II) irradiated by a UV lamp (365nm) while appeared slightly transparent under daylight (photograph I). Again, the UV–vis absorption spectrum showed a peak at 335nm owing to n–π⁎ transition of C=O (Fig. S2). At the same time, we applied the CDs as fluorescent ink for interestingly drawing (Fig. S1). The surface functional groups of the CDs were validated by Fourier Transform Infrared spectrometer (FTIR). As shown in Fig. 2B, the absorption band appearing at 3421cm−1 was assigned to stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H. Meanwhile, the absorption band appearing at 2309cm−1 referring to the stretching vibrations of CC. Moreover, the band at 1638cm−1 was related with the stretching vibrations of C=O or C=C, however, the UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. S2) displayed a peak at 335nm originated from n–π⁎ transition of C=O, indicating there was C=O only. The peaks in the range 1000–1400cm−1 indicated the existence of O–H and C–O groups. Taken together, these data reveal there existing O–H, N–H, C≡C, C=O and C–O groups on the surface of the CDs described here. Consequently, vancomycin can be modified on the surfaces of CDs by –COOH and –NH2, while EDC and NHS function as the crosslinking agents. For further investigation of the nanostructures of CDs, HR-TEM was employed to directly observe the morphology and particle size distributions (Fig. 2C), and these CDs were spherical and well-dispersed. For the size distribution analysis, the diameter of CDs existed in the range of 2.2±0.3nm confirmed by DLS (Fig. 2D). All the above data claimed a successful synthesis of functional CDs, providing the CDs' possibility for further experiments.

To achieve specific sensing, vancomycin was then modified on the surfaces of CDs through the condensation reaction of amine and carboxyl, in which EDC and NHS functioned as crosslinking agents. As shown in Fig. 3A, the fluorescence intensity of CD@Van showed scarce variation either in the photograph or fluorescent spectra compared with that of CDs themselves. Meanwhile, the FTIR spectrum of CD@Van was performed for understanding their functional groups (Fig. 3B). Compared with that of CDs and vancomycin (Fig. S3), the characteristic absorption peaks of CDs still appeared, and the additional absorption peaks were originated from the existence of functional groups of vancomycin. Moreover, the similar results were observed about the UV–vis absorption spectra. As revealed in Fig. S2, the CDs showed an absorption peak at 335nm. Meanwhile, vancomycin showed an absorption peak at 275nm originated from π–π⁎ transition of C=C while CD@Van implied two obvious peaks referring to CDs and vancomycin. We also have employed HR-TEM for characterization of CD@Van. As shown in Fig. 3C, there is almost no obvious change on the morphology and size between CDs and CD@Van. All these evidence proved that vancomycin hardly implied any influence on the properties of the CDs once being conjugated with them, and this modification provided the possibility of the modified CDs for sensing bacteria. In addition, MS has been applied for determining the core size of the resulted Cu nanoclusters (CuNCs) (Ghosh et al., 2014) and Au nanoclusters (AuNCs) (Yang et al., 2011) according to their molecular weight of CuNCs and AuNCs and their templates. Therefore, we have employed MS to identify the grafting density of CD@Van. As shown in Fig. S4A, the dominant m/z peaks at 909.3, 724.4 and 493.6 were originated from three types of different molecular weight of CDs, and other neighbor peaks were assigned to their related fragments that lost functional groups on the CDs' surfaces such as NH2, CH3, and OH. Meanwhile, the obvious m/z peak at 1450.5 (Fig. S4B) was originated from [Van+H]+, and the m/z peaks at 1332.2 and 1122.1 were attributed to [Van–C4H8O2N2–H]+ and [Van–C16H16O6N2]+ respectively. Moreover, the m/z peak at 3808.1 (Fig. 3D) demonstrated that there existed 2 vancomycin modified on the surfaces of CDs on the basis of the calculation: 3808≈2×1449+909. Meanwhile, the m/z peak at 3476.8 was attributed to [CD@2Van–C16H16O6N2]+, and the m/z peak at 2714.2 was referred to [CD@2Van–2C16H16O6N2–C7H16ON–C4H8O2N2+H]+, and the m/z peaks at 1448.5 and 907.4 were assigned to vancomycin and CD splitting from CD@Van respectively, further confirming the previous modification descriptions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Building up this assay and its mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: Towards constructing an innovative assay, we addressed whether this CD@Van can be applied for detecting bacteria or not. Consequently, S. aureus were directly introduced into CD@Van, and dramatic fluorescence decrease was observed ( Fig. 4A), indicating their availability for detecting S. aureus. Next, serving as the control experiment, the CDs without modifications were mixed with S. aureus in PBS (20mM, pH 7.4). As shown in Fig. 4B, the fluorescence intensity of CDs varied slightly as varied concentrations of S. aureus added, showing no direct interactions between S. aureus and CDs without vancomycin. As a result, CD@Van played the role as a probe for S. aureus rather than CDs. Since vancomycin exhibited high affinity toward multiple d-Ala-d-Ala receptors (Rao et al., 1999), the reasonable explanation for the decreased fluorescence of CD@Van is that multi-CDs aggregated on the surfaces of S. aureus (Fig. 4C and D) due to vancomycin binding with the terminal peptides of d-Ala-d-Ala by high avidity of ligand–receptor interactions (Gu et al., 2003). In addition, different pH, temperature and varied concentrations of NaCl were introduced to explore the effect on the aggregations of CDs and CD@Van (Figs. S6, S7, and S8), demonstrating occurring of aggregations was not induced by those factors.

To visualize the interactions between CD@Van and S. aureus, further related experiments were designed. Briefly, S. aureus were diluted as 1000 (Fig. S9A, C, and E) and 10000 (Fig. S9B, D, and F) folds respectively and subjected to plate counting. In Fig. S9A and B, the S. aureus samples without CDs or CD@Van added served as controls, showing their normal growth. Likewise, there existed slight decrease for the amount of bacterial colonies while S. aureus was incubated with CDs at 37°C for 50min (Fig. S9C and D), demonstrating that the CDs without modifications hardly interacted with S. aureus and their acceptable low-toxicity. In contrast, S. aureus by mixing with CD@Van (Fig. S9E and F) led to a distinct decrease for the amount of bacteria colonies compared with that in Fig. S9A and B respectively. This result indicated that decreased bacteria were caused by the interactions between vancomycin of CD@Van and S. aureus, and this modification showed scarce effect on the antibiotic property of vancomycin. Taken together, these photographs directly described that CD@Van can interact with bacteria by vancomycin on their surfaces, which facilitated to induce the decrease of S. aureus colonies.

For optimizing detection conditions, factors possibly affecting were explored. As the amount of vancomycin may play a critical role to obtain the maximal decrease of the fluorescence intensity, different concentrations of vancomycin were introduced during synthesizing CD@Van. As shown in Fig. S10A, 10mg/mL of vancomycin was proved as the optimized amount. Likewise, considering the possible influence of incubation time on the detecting procedures, varying time (0min, 10min, 20min, 30min, 40min, 50min, 60min, 70min, 80min and 90min) for reacting between CD@Van and S. aureus has been also studied. In Fig. S10B, the fluorescence of CD@Van constantly decreased while the reaction time was less than 50min, because the reaction processes have not finished and there still existed a certain amount of S. aureus. Howbeit, the fluorescence decrease of CD@Van reached the maximum once the reaction time was more than 50min, suggesting the reactions completed. Thus, 50min serves as the optimal reaction time. Eventually, 10mg/mL of vancomycin and 50min of incubation time were identified as the optimal conditions for the following explorations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of S. aureus by using CD@Van
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the designed strategy, the standard cure of this method was established by various concentrations of S. aureus (3.18×105, 6.36×105, 1.59×106, 3.18×106, 6.36×106, 1.59×107, 3.18×107, 6.36×107, and 1.59×108 cfu/mL). The results ( Fig. 5A and B) indicated that the fluorescence intensity of CD@Van decreased proportionally with varied amounts of S. aureus added. Particularly, the fluorescence intensity decrease versus the logarithmic plots of S. aureus concentrations displayed a linear range of 3.18×105–1.59×108 cfu/mL together with its correlation coefficient of 0.976, and the detection limit was 9.40×104 cfu/mL at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3, suggesting its sensitivity and promising. Hence, the data obtained here have broadened avenues for determining S. aureus.

As S. aureus being a member of Gram-positive bacteria, we conjectured whether other similar bacteria exhibited interactions with CD@Van or not. Thereby, B. subtitles and L. monocytogenes were chosen as the alternatives, and different concentrations of the two types of bacteria were separately mixed with CD@Van and followed by incubation for 50min. For B. subtitles ( Fig. 5 C and D), the fluorescence intensity decrease of CD@Van versus the logarithmic plots of B. subtitles concentrations similarly displayed a linear range of 4.22×105–2.11×108 cfu/mL along with a correlation coefficient of 0.986, and its detection limit was 1.03×105 cfu/mL. Likewise, Fig. 5E and F indicated CD@Van can also serve as probes for detecting L. monocytogenes in a linear range of 3.34×105–1.67×108 cfu/mL with a correlation coefficient of 0.992, and its detection limit was 9.84×104 cfu/mL. Taken together, the data above demonstrated that the current method based on CD@Van showed interactions with Gram-positive bacteria rather than any one of the others.

Towards evaluating the selectivity of CD@Van, three typical Gram-negative bacteria of Salmonella, P. aeruginosa and E. coli were separately introduced into CD@Van. As shown in Fig. S11, dramatic decrease of their fluorescence was observed for CD@Van upon additions of S. aureus, B. subtilis and L. monocytogenes, indicating their sensitive responses to Gram-positive bacteria. In striking contrast, there was no obvious decrease appeared by adding other Gram-negative bacteria into the CD@Van, revealing the satisfactory selectivity of the proposed method for sensing Gram-positive bacteria.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis S. aureus in actual sample
ce:section.ce:para: To validate its applicability, this approach was employed to detect S. aureus for exploring its recovery. In particular, S. aureus in orange juice was incubated at 37°C for different time to obtain various concentrations of bacterial suspension, and the corresponding results were derived from the standard curve and regression equation. Importantly, their recoveries obtained were above 90% in all cases as listed in Table 1, suggesting the generally satisfactory recovery and RSD. Meanwhile, the traditional plate-counting method for assaying different concentrations of S. aureus in orange juice served as the controls (Fig. S12). These data claimed that the modified CDs here revealed potential for broadening avenues of detecting target bacteria in actual samples.

In summary, we hereby creatively took advantage of the fluorescent CDs to build up an assay towards detecting Gram-positive bacteria. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report of bacteria detection originated from CDs. To be specific, aggregations of CD@Van occurred on the surfaces of bacteria based on the ligand–receptor interactions, thereby leading to their fluorescence decrease. Significantly, the linear range of 3.18×105–1.59×108 cfu/mL for S. aureus was basically acceptable, indicating that this approach has provided a new way for determining target bacteria in complex samples. Nevertheless, the current method showed the only weakness of CD@Van exhibiting the selectivity to Gram-positive bacteria rather than one specific bacterium of them, due to the broad-spectrum of vancomycin. Meaningfully, we have developed an outstanding application for CDs, and look forward to applying this contribution for nanomedical and clinic purpose by virtue of the future improvement.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge financial support by Research Fund for the Doctoral Program of Higher Education of China (20110182120014), Natural Science Foundation Project of CQ CSTC (cstc2013jcyjA10117), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (XDJK2015A005 and 2362014xk07), and Program for Innovative Research Team in University of Chongqing (2013).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2015.07.015.
